summary:
The cribriform plate is the most common site of CSF rhinorrhea due to its anatomical vulnerability—being thin and porous—and frequent involvement during head injuries or nasal surgeries. This location directly connects the cranial cavity with the paranasal sinuses via small openings, allowing cerebrospinal fluid to leak into the nose when fractured.

We are only asked to summarize Agent1’s reasoning — not rephrase a general answer.

Final summary:

The cribriform plate is the most common site of CSF rhinorhée because it is thin, porous, and prone to fracture from trauma or surgery. It serves as an entry point between the brain and nasopharynx through which CSF can escape if damaged.
